input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, comprising a motor, gearbox, and Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller. Operating in a manufacturing environment, this system's precise motion control and real-time data acquisition capabilities make it suitable for digital twinning to enable monitoring, optimization, and predictive maintenance.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin system employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for abstraction levels. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs model components, interfaces, and interconnections. Behavior is described using MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, with further detail achieved via MathWorks Simulink, simulation tools, and programming languages like C++ and Java.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1) is a UML class diagram defining components including Purpose, DataTrace, Asset, Processing, and Model. The concrete implementation integrates mechanical structure modeling, friction behavior simulation, Asset Administration Shells (AAS), live-data linking from the physical robot, and dashboard visualization for real-time monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: MBSE employs SysML and ADLs (e.g., AADL, MontiArc) to model system components, interfaces, and interconnections across abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical). Behavioral models utilize state machines in SysML v1.7 and MontiArc Automaton, while analytical models leverage MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java). Data management integrates heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives across multiple modeling tools and notations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical acting components include a motor driving the axis, a controller for operation management, and safety constraints limiting actuation range to prevent mechanical damage or unsafe conditions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure comprises position encoders spatially distributed at the motor and gearbox, providing real-time feedback with high precision to reduce uncertainty in system state estimation.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is validated by comparing simulated outputs with measured physical data; uncertainty is quantified and reduced through optimized sensor arrangement, with continuous improvement cycles for model accuracy and reliability.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series data including input current and output angle from position encoders is transmitted via MQTT protocol. Transmission frequencies, event triggers, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, and data integration mechanisms are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication via MQTT protocol for real-time data streaming. Network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, and security protocols are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process for creating digital shadows involves structured steps including problem identification, asset and model analysis, use or creation of calculations/models, and data identification. During operation, models are continuously improved through iterative refinement based on real-time data feedback, ensuring alignment with physical system behavior. This evolution is supported by methodologies that integrate data-driven updates with existing system models.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support capabilities include real-time visualization in dashboards for user interaction, with insights generated through analytics such as identification of joint friction in robotic systems. These insights enable actionable recommendations to improve motion accuracy, with confidence measures derived from data-driven validation against physical system performance metrics.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Relevant industry standards include SysML for model-based systems engineering, AADL for architecture description, ACME as a framework for architecture description, MontiArc for component-based modeling, SPES for embedded systems engineering, RFLP for requirements, functions, logic, physics modeling, and OOSEM for object-oriented software engineering methods. These standards are applied across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer) to ensure structured system modeling and interoperability.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the specific robot model (MABI MAX 100), its components (motor, gearbox), and controller (Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl), which are explicitly detailed in Section III. It correctly captures the manufacturing context and the focus on motion control. However, it includes 'predictive maintenance' as a goal, which is not mentioned in the text (the text focuses on 'motion accuracy' and 'friction identification'). Supported by quotes: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'consisting of the motor and gearbox', 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'.

The extraction claims 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly mentions 'visualization in a dashboard' and the display of data in the dashboard as part of the digital shadow implementation. While the list of services is limited, stating that nothing is present is incorrect based on the evidence.

The extraction provides a comprehensive list of tools and methodologies mentioned in the text, specifically from Section II.A 'Modeling Software and Systems'. It correctly identifies MBSE methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM), Architecture Description Languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML), and behavior description tools (Simulink, C++, Java). While the text presents some methodologies like RFLP as general examples rather than explicitly employed by the specific use case (which relies heavily on SysML and Simulink), the extraction accurately captures the technological context and enablers discussed.
